__meta.note: Complete seed database for a fresh FlockOS church deployment. Import via: Admin → Church Audit → ⬆ Import .json "churches" collection is backend-only — skipped on import. Content collections (books, theology, missions, etc.) are top-level Firestore collections. Church-operational collections (members, sermons, etc.) start empty. Strong's concordances omitted (run without --no-strongs to include).

books.author: Moses
books.summary: The book of beginnings. It details the creation of the world, the fall of man, the flood, the Tower of Babel, and God’s covenant with Abraham and his descendants.
books.application: Genesis lays the foundation for all Scripture. It affirms God’s sovereignty as Creator, introduces the problem of sin, and establishes the covenant promises that find their fulfillment in Christ.
books.genre: The Books of the Law (The Pentateuch)

books.author: Moses
books.summary: God delivers the Israelites from slavery in Egypt, establishes His covenant with them at Sinai, and gives them the Law.
books.application: Demonstrates God’s power to save and His desire to dwell with His people. Encourages the Church to walk in obedience and trust in His deliverance.
books.genre: The Books of the Law (The Pentateuch)

books.author: Moses
books.summary: Contains laws regarding offerings, cleanliness, holiness, and the priesthood to guide Israel’s worship and conduct.
books.application: Teaches the holiness of God and the need for a mediator—pointing to Christ as our High Priest and ultimate sacrifice.
books.genre: The Books of the Law (The Pentateuch)

books.author: Moses
books.summary: Chronicles Israel’s wilderness journey, highlighting their rebellion and God’s faithfulness.
books.application: A sobering reminder of the dangers of unbelief and the necessity of trusting God’s guidance and provision.
books.genre: The Books of the Law (The Pentateuch)

books.author: Moses
books.summary: Moses’ final sermons to Israel before entering the Promised Land, urging covenant faithfulness.
books.application: Reinforces the call to love God wholeheartedly and live in obedience to His Word. Reminds the Church to remember God’s past faithfulness.
books.genre: The Books of the Law (The Pentateuch)

books.author: Joshua
books.summary: Israel’s conquest and division of the Promised Land.
books.application: Emphasizes God’s faithfulness to His promises and the need for courage, faith, and obedience in spiritual battles.

books.author: Traditionally attributed to Samuel
books.summary: Israel falls into cycles of sin, oppression, repentance, and deliverance through God-appointed judges.
books.application: Warns against moral relativism and forgetting God. Encourages spiritual vigilance and repentance.

books.author: Traditionally attributed to Samuel
books.summary: A story of loyalty, redemption, and God’s providence through the lives of Ruth and Boaz.
books.application: Illustrates God’s sovereignty in ordinary lives and foreshadows Christ as our Kinsman-Redeemer.

books.author: Samuel, with additions by Gad and Nathan
books.summary: Covers the transition from judges to kings, focusing on Samuel, Saul, and David.
books.application: Shows that true leadership is rooted in obedience to God. Warns of the consequences of pride and disobedience.

books.author: Gad and Nathan
books.summary: Chronicles the reign of David, including his victories, sins, and God’s covenant with him.
books.application: Demonstrates both the rewards of faithfulness and the consequences of sin. Points to the promised Messiah from David’s line.

books.author: Traditionally attributed to Jeremiah
books.summary: Recounts Solomon’s reign, the building of the Temple, and the division of the kingdom.
books.application: Teaches the importance of finishing well and remaining faithful. Warns against idolatry and compromise.

books.author: Traditionally attributed to Jeremiah
books.summary: Details the kings of Israel and Judah leading up to their exile due to persistent sin.
books.application: Reveals God’s justice and mercy. Calls the Church to repent and remain faithful amidst moral decline.

books.author: Traditionally attributed to Ezra
books.summary: Focuses on genealogies and the reign of David with a priestly perspective.
books.application: Emphasizes worship, the temple, and the Davidic covenant, encouraging reverence for God’s presence.

books.author: Traditionally attributed to Ezra
books.summary: Focuses on the kings of Judah and the centrality of temple worship.
books.application: Reinforces the importance of worship, repentance, and godly leadership.

books.author: Ezra
books.summary: Records the return from Babylonian exile and the rebuilding of the temple.
books.application: Shows God’s faithfulness to restore. Encourages the Church to prioritize spiritual renewal.

books.author: Nehemiah
books.summary: Tells how Jerusalem’s walls were rebuilt amid opposition, and how the people recommitted to the covenant.
books.application: Highlights the power of prayer, leadership, and community commitment to God’s work.

books.author: Unknown (possibly Mordecai)
books.summary: God’s providential care for His people through Queen Esther’s courage.
books.application: Encourages trust in God’s sovereignty even when He seems silent. Reminds believers they are placed for a purpose.

books.author: Unknown (possibly Moses or Solomon)
books.summary: Explores the problem of suffering and God’s sovereignty through Job’s trials.
books.application: Teaches the Church to trust in God’s wisdom and character even in suffering.

books.author: David and others
books.summary: A collection of songs and prayers expressing the full range of human emotion toward God.
books.application: Encourages honest and passionate worship, lament, and thanksgiving. Helps believers draw near to God in every season.

books.author: Solomon and others
books.summary: Short sayings and instructions for wise, godly living.
books.application: Provides practical guidance rooted in the fear of the Lord. Calls the Church to integrity, diligence, and humility.

books.author: Solomon
books.summary: A philosophical reflection on the vanity of life without God.
books.application: Reminds believers that meaning and satisfaction are found only in revering God and living for Him.

books.author: Solomon
books.summary: A poetic celebration of love between a bride and groom.
books.application: Affirms the beauty of marital love and can be seen as an allegory of Christ’s love for His Bride, the Church.

books.author: Isaiah
books.summary: A prophetic book warning Judah of impending judgment, while offering hope of restoration and the coming Messiah.
books.application: Calls the Church to holiness, reveals God’s redemptive plan, and proclaims the suffering and reign of Christ.

books.author: Jeremiah
books.summary: Warnings to Judah of coming exile due to sin, mixed with messages of hope and a new covenant.
books.application: Teaches the cost of unrepentant sin, God’s patience, and His promise of heart transformation through the New Covenant.

books.author: Jeremiah
books.summary: Poetic laments over the destruction of Jerusalem.
books.application: Helps believers process grief, mourn over sin, and cling to God’s mercy in sorrow.

books.author: Ezekiel
books.summary: Visions, symbolic acts, and prophecies declaring judgment on Israel and surrounding nations, with hope of future restoration.
books.application: Reminds the Church of God’s holiness, justice, and promise to renew His people through the Spirit.

books.author: Daniel
books.summary: Stories of faithfulness in exile and apocalyptic visions of God’s kingdom triumphing over earthly empires.
books.application: Encourages steadfastness in hostile cultures and confidence in God’s sovereign rule over history.

books.author: Hosea
books.summary: God uses Hosea’s marriage to an unfaithful wife to illustrate Israel’s spiritual adultery.
books.application: Displays God’s relentless love and calls the Church to faithful devotion.

books.author: Joel
books.summary: A locust plague as a picture of judgment and a call to repentance, with a promise of the outpouring of the Spirit.
books.application: Encourages repentance and reminds the Church of the fulfilled and future work of the Holy Spirit.

books.author: Amos
books.summary: A shepherd-turned-prophet condemns social injustice and religious hypocrisy in Israel.
books.application: Urges the Church to pursue justice, righteousness, and sincere worship.

books.author: Obadiah
books.summary: A prophecy against Edom for pride and violence toward Judah.
books.application: Warns of pride and assures believers that God will vindicate His people.

books.author: Jonah
books.summary: A reluctant prophet sent to preach repentance to Nineveh; a lesson in God’s mercy.
books.application: Shows God’s heart for the nations and challenges the Church to obey and show compassion.

books.author: Micah
books.summary: Judgment on sin and promises of a future kingdom under a righteous ruler.
books.application: Calls for justice, mercy, and humility, and points to Jesus as the coming Shepherd-King.

books.author: Nahum
books.summary: A prophecy of Nineveh’s destruction due to its return to wickedness.
books.application: Reminds the Church that God is both patient and just in dealing with sin.

books.author: Habakkuk
books.summary: A dialogue between the prophet and God about injustice and God’s use of Babylon for judgment.
books.application: Encourages believers to live by faith and trust God’s justice even when circumstances are unclear.

books.author: Zephaniah
books.summary: Prophesies the coming “Day of the Lord” and calls for repentance.
books.application: Warns of judgment but promises joy and restoration to the humble and faithful.

books.author: Haggai
books.summary: Calls the returned exiles to rebuild the temple and prioritize God’s house.
books.application: Encourages the Church to put God’s Kingdom first and remain faithful in the work of ministry.

books.author: Zechariah
books.summary: Visions and prophecies urging temple rebuilding and promising the coming Messiah.
books.application: Points to Jesus as the Priest-King and inspires hope in God’s future promises.

books.author: Malachi
books.summary: Addresses the spiritual apathy of post-exilic Israel and foretells the coming of the “messenger.”
books.application: Challenges the Church to honor God in worship and expect Christ’s return with faithful hearts.

books.author: Matthew (Levi), the tax collector and disciple
books.summary: Presents Jesus as the promised Messiah and King of the Jews, fulfilling Old Testament prophecy.
books.application: Calls the Church to embrace Christ as King and live under His Kingdom values—making disciples of all nations.

books.author: John Mark
books.summary: A fast-paced account of Jesus’ ministry, emphasizing His authority and servanthood.
books.application: Encourages believers to follow Jesus with urgency, humility, and bold faith.

books.author: Luke, a physician and companion of Paul
books.summary: A detailed and orderly account of Jesus’ life, ministry, and compassion, emphasizing His humanity and mission to the marginalized.
books.application: Inspires the Church to reach the lost, care for the outcast, and grow in certainty about the Gospel.

books.author: John, the beloved disciple
books.summary: Highlights the deity of Christ through signs and personal encounters to lead people to faith.
books.application: Strengthens the Church’s belief in Jesus as the Son of God and the only way to eternal life.

books.author: Luke
books.summary: Chronicles the birth and expansion of the early Church through the power of the Holy Spirit.
books.application: Demonstrates how the Spirit empowers believers to be witnesses. Encourages the Church to remain Spirit-led and Gospel-centered.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: A theological masterpiece explaining the righteousness of God, justification by faith, and the Gospel’s implications.
books.application: Grounds the Church in sound doctrine and calls for a transformed life of worship and unity.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: Addresses division, immorality, spiritual gifts, and resurrection in the Corinthian church.
books.application: Challenges the Church to pursue holiness, unity, and love while maintaining order in worship.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: A defense of Paul’s ministry and a call to authentic Christian living amid weakness.
books.application: Encourages the Church to find strength in weakness and maintain integrity in leadership.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: Defends the Gospel of grace against legalism and affirms justification by faith.
books.application: Calls the Church to freedom in Christ and life in the Spirit.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: Explores the Church’s identity in Christ and the mystery of the Gospel.
books.application: Strengthens the Church in unity, spiritual maturity, and readiness for spiritual battle.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: A letter of joy, written from prison, encouraging partnership in the Gospel.
books.application: Inspires the Church to rejoice in all circumstances and press on toward Christlikeness.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: Affirms Christ’s supremacy over all creation and the sufficiency of the Gospel.
books.application: Encourages believers to remain rooted in Christ and avoid deception.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: Commends faithfulness and clarifies hope in Christ’s return.
books.application: Urges the Church to live in readiness and encourage one another.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: Addresses misunderstandings about Christ’s return and encourages perseverance.
books.application: Reinforces hope, warns against idleness, and affirms the certainty of judgment and victory.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: Instructions for church leadership, sound doctrine, and godly conduct.
books.application: Equips the Church with guidelines for healthy pastoral ministry and spiritual oversight.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: Paul’s final letter, urging faithfulness to the Word amid suffering.
books.application: Inspires leaders to remain steadfast, preach the Word, and fulfill their calling.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: Instructions for appointing elders and promoting good works in the Church.
books.application: Encourages sound teaching and godly living that reflects the Gospel.

books.author: Paul
books.summary: A personal plea for grace and reconciliation between a master and his slave.
books.application: Promotes forgiveness and unity in Christ that transcends social boundaries.

books.author: Unknown (traditionally attributed to Paul or another early leader)
books.summary: Declares Christ as superior to the Old Covenant system—our High Priest and perfect sacrifice.
books.application: Strengthens believers to persevere in faith and draws attention to the sufficiency of Christ.

books.author: James, the brother of Jesus
books.summary: Emphasizes living faith through action, wisdom, and righteous conduct.
books.application: Challenges the Church to demonstrate genuine faith through obedience and love.

books.author: Peter
books.summary: Encouragement to suffering believers to remain holy and hopeful.
books.application: Calls the Church to live as exiles in a hostile world with courage and holiness.

books.author: Peter
books.summary: Warns against false teachers and reminds of the Lord’s return.
books.application: Encourages growth in knowledge, spiritual discernment, and patient endurance.

books.author: John
books.summary: Affirms Jesus as fully God and man, and calls for love, obedience, and truth.

books.author: John
books.summary: A brief warning against false teachers denying Christ.
books.application: Stresses the need to uphold truth and avoid partnering with heresy.

books.author: John
books.summary: Commends hospitality and warns against prideful leadership.
books.application: Encourages godly service and humility in Church relationships.

books.author: Jude, the brother of Jesus
books.summary: A call to contend for the faith and resist false teachers.
books.application: Urges vigilance, doctrinal soundness, and dependence on God’s grace.

books.author: John
books.summary: A prophetic vision of Christ’s ultimate victory, the judgment of evil, and the new heavens and new earth.
books.application: Inspires hope, holiness, and perseverance. Reminds the Church that Jesus reigns and is coming soon…and very soon!

genealogy.name: Aaron
genealogy.bio: Brother of Moses, spokesman, and first High Priest of Israel.
genealogy.title: The First High Priest

genealogy.name: Abagtha
genealogy.bio: In esther 1:10, court official of king ahasuerus.

genealogy.name: Abda
genealogy.bio: In 1 kings 4:6, father of adoniram.

genealogy.name: Abdeel
genealogy.bio: In jeremiah 36:26, father of shelemiah.

genealogy.name: Abdi
genealogy.bio: Male biblical protagonist in 2 chronicles 29:12, father of kish, spouse of azariah.

genealogy.name: Abdi, son of Malluch
genealogy.bio: Male biblical protagonist in 1 chronicles 6:44, son of malluch the son of hashabiah.

genealogy.name: Abdi-el, son of Guni
genealogy.bio: Male biblical protagonist in 1 chronicles 5:15, son of guni.

genealogy.name: Abdiel
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure (first book of chronicles).

genealogy.name: Abdon
genealogy.bio: In judges 12:13-15, judge of israel. Parents: Hillel the Pirathonite.

genealogy.name: Abednego
genealogy.bio: One of the three youths thrown into the furnace in the book of daniel (book of daniel).

genealogy.name: Abel
genealogy.bio: A keeper of sheep. Offered a pleasing sacrifice to God. Murdered by Cain.
genealogy.title: The Righteous Shepherd

genealogy.name: Abi-Albon
genealogy.bio: In 2 samuel 23:31 and 1 chronicles 11:32, one of david's 'thirty' mighty warriors.

genealogy.name: Abiah
genealogy.bio: In 1 chronicles 2:24, wife of hezron, mother of ashur.

genealogy.name: Abiasaph
genealogy.bio: Male biblical figure, in exodus 6:24, son of korah, father of assir, ancestor of samuel. Parents: Korah.

genealogy.name: Abiathar
genealogy.bio: In 1 samuel 22:20-23 &c, high priest at nob. Parents: Ahimelech.

genealogy.name: Abida
genealogy.bio: In genesis 25:4 and 1 chronicles 1:33, son of midian. Parents: Midian.

genealogy.name: Abidan
genealogy.bio: Human male biblical figure in numbers 1:11 &c, judge, son of gideoni. Parents: Gideoni.

genealogy.name: Abiel
genealogy.bio: In 1 samuel 9:1, son of zeror the son of bechorath. Parents: Tseror.

genealogy.name: Abiezek
genealogy.bio: Man reviewing the israelites for saul according to the septuagint.

genealogy.name: Abiezer
genealogy.bio: Male biblical figure, in joshua 17:2 &c., male descendant of manasseh son of joseph. Parents: Hepher.

genealogy.name: Abigail
genealogy.bio: Person in bible, sister of david, mother of amasa. Parents: Jesse, Nitzevet.

genealogy.name: Abihail
genealogy.bio: Female biblical figure in 1 chronicles 2:29, wife of abishur the son of shammai.

genealogy.name: Abihu
genealogy.bio: Human biblical character (exodus). Parents: Aaron, Elisheba.

genealogy.name: Abihud
genealogy.bio: Son of bela and a descendant of benjamin.

genealogy.name: Abijah (Abijam)
genealogy.bio: Ruled for three years, continuing war with the Northern Kingdom.
genealogy.title: King of Judah

genealogy.name: Abimelech
genealogy.bio: In judges 8:31, king of shechem. Parents: Gideon.

genealogy.name: Abinadab
genealogy.bio: In 1 samuel 16:8, son of jesse the son of obed. Parents: Jesse, Nitzevet.

genealogy.name: Abinadab of Kiriath-jearim
genealogy.bio: Father of eleazar.

genealogy.name: Abinoam
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure (judges).

genealogy.name: Abiram
genealogy.bio: In numbers 16:1, son of eliab. Parents: Eliab.

genealogy.name: Abishai
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure. Parents: Zeruiah.

genealogy.name: Abishur
genealogy.bio: In 1 chronicles 2:28, son of shammai, brother of nadab. Parents: Shammai.

genealogy.name: Abital
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure.

genealogy.name: Abner
genealogy.bio: Biblical character. Parents: Ner.

genealogy.name: Abra
genealogy.bio: Biblical character, maid of judith of the book of judith.

genealogy.name: Abraham
genealogy.bio: Called by God to leave his homeland of Ur. Received the covenant promises of land, seed, and blessing.
genealogy.children: ishmael, isaac, zimran, jokshan, medan, midian, ishbak, shuah
genealogy.title: Father of Multitudes

genealogy.name: Absalom
genealogy.bio: Biblical character; third son of david (second book of samuel). Parents: David, Maacah.

genealogy.name: Achaicus of Corinth
genealogy.bio: One of the seventy disciples.

genealogy.name: Achan
genealogy.bio: Biblical character (joshua 7); executed by stoning. Parents: Carmi.

genealogy.name: Achbor
genealogy.bio: Male biblical character in 2 kings 22:12, whose father was micah.

genealogy.name: Achinoam
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure (first book of samuel). Parents: Ahimaaz.
genealogy.children: Jonathan, Merab, Abinadab ben Saul, Michal, Ish-bosheth

genealogy.name: Achior
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure (book of judith).

genealogy.name: Achish
genealogy.bio: Biblical character (first book of samuel). Parents: Maoch.

genealogy.name: Achsah
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure ; achsah is mentioned as the daughter of caleb, the son of hur, in the genealogy of the tribe of judah. Parents: Caleb.

genealogy.name: Adah
genealogy.bio: In genesis 4:19, wife of lamech.

genealogy.name: Adaiah
genealogy.bio: Male biblical figure in ezra 10:39, descendant of binnui.

genealogy.name: Adam
genealogy.bio: The first human created by God from the dust of the earth. Placed in the Garden of Eden.
genealogy.title: The First Man

genealogy.name: Adar
genealogy.bio: Son of patriarch benjamin or his son bela according to the septuagint.

genealogy.name: Addi
genealogy.bio: Father of melchi, son of cosam (gospel of luke). Parents: Cosam.

genealogy.name: Adinah
genealogy.bio: Daughter of jobab.

genealogy.name: Adinon the Asonite
genealogy.bio: One of king david's valiant men.

genealogy.name: Admatha
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, advisor to xerxes.

genealogy.name: Admin
genealogy.bio: Other father of ammindab, son of arni, only mentioned in nlt version. Parents: Arni.

genealogy.name: Adna
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, a priest who served during the time of joiakim, the son of jeshua, the high priest who returned from the babylonian exile.

genealogy.name: Adnah
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, one of the manassite leaders who defected to david at ziklag while he was hiding from saul.

genealogy.name: Adonijah
genealogy.bio: Biblical character. Parents: David, Haggith.

genealogy.name: Adoniram
genealogy.bio: In 1 kings 2:6 and 2 samuel 20:24, son of abda. Parents: Abda.

genealogy.name: Adonizedek
genealogy.bio: In joshua 10:1-3, amorite king of jerusalem.

genealogy.name: Adriel
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure. Parents: Barzilai the Giladi.

genealogy.name: Aedis
genealogy.bio: Son of the patriarch gad according to the septuagint.

genealogy.name: Aeneas
genealogy.bio: Biblical character in the new testament (acts 9).

genealogy.name: Agag
genealogy.bio: In 1 samuel 15:8-33, king of the amalekites.

genealogy.name: Agee
genealogy.bio: Hararite ; shammah's father.

genealogy.name: Agur
genealogy.bio: Son of Jakeh. Authored a chapter of wisdom literature noted for its humility and numeric sayings.
genealogy.title: Author of Proverbs 30

genealogy.name: Ahasbai
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure (second book of samuel).

genealogy.name: Ahasuerus
genealogy.bio: Name of one or more kings of persia in the hebrew bible (esther, ezra, daniel), cognate to the greek form xerxes or artaxerxes.

genealogy.name: Ahaz
genealogy.bio: An exceedingly wicked king who sacrificed his sons in the fire and defiled the temple.
genealogy.title: King of Judah

genealogy.name: Ahaziah
genealogy.bio: Ruled one year. Killed by Jehu during the purge of Ahab's house.
genealogy.title: King of Judah

genealogy.name: Ahban
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure in 1 chronicles 2:29, son of abishur and abihail, brother of molid. Parents: Abishur, Abihail.

genealogy.name: Aher
genealogy.bio: A son of shemer, a descendant of asher.

genealogy.name: Ahi
genealogy.bio: Son of abdiel, was a chief of the gadites. Parents: Abdiel.

genealogy.name: Ahiam
genealogy.bio: One of king david's valiant men.

genealogy.name: Ahiel
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure.

genealogy.name: Ahiezer
genealogy.bio: Chief of the tribe of dan at the time of the first census of the israelites in the desert. Parents: Ammishaddai.

genealogy.name: Ahiezer son of Shemaah of Gibeah
Ahihud
genealogy.bio: Son of shelomi and a leader of the tribe of asher at the time of the partition of the land of canaan. Parents: Shelomi.

genealogy.name: Ahijah
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure ; son of ahitub. Parents: Ahitub.

genealogy.name: Ahijah the Shilonite
genealogy.bio: Biblical prophet in the old testament (first book of kings). Parents: http://www.wikidata.org/.well-known/genid/a508bf269d03455dfe4dfd66df59990b.

genealogy.name: Ahikam
genealogy.bio: Biblical character. Parents: Shaphan.

genealogy.name: Ahilud
genealogy.bio: Jehoshaphat's father.

genealogy.name: Ahimaaz
genealogy.bio: Ahimaaz, who was married to solomon's daughter basemath, served as the governor of naphtali during solomon's reign.

genealogy.name: Ahiman
genealogy.bio: Ahiman was a levite who served as a gatekeeper during the reign of david.

genealogy.name: Ahimelech
genealogy.bio: Biblical character (first book of samuel). Parents: Ahitub.

genealogy.name: Ahinadab
genealogy.bio: Ahinadab, the son of iddo, was one of solomon's twelve governors, responsible for providing provisions for the king's household.

genealogy.name: Ahinoam
genealogy.reference: Jewish Encyclopedia of Brockhaus and Efron
genealogy.bio: Biblical character, wife of david.

genealogy.name: Ahio
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, driver of the cart carrying the ark of god from kiriath-jearim.

genealogy.name: Ahira
genealogy.bio: Hebrew bible character ; chief of the tribe of naphtali in the desert. Parents: Enan.

genealogy.name: Ahisamach
genealogy.bio: Father of ooliab (tribe of dan).

genealogy.name: Ahishahar
genealogy.bio: Son of bilhan and descendant of benjamin.

genealogy.name: Ahishar
genealogy.bio: Overseer of solomon's household.

genealogy.name: Ahitophel
genealogy.bio: Biblical character.

genealogy.name: Ahitub
genealogy.bio: Aaron descendant, high priest (old testament). Parents: Phinehas.

genealogy.name: Ahitub II
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure in 1 chronicles 6:12, father of zadok ii, son of amariah ii. Parents: Amariah II.

genealogy.name: Ahlai
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure in 1 chronicles 2:31, daughter or son of sheshan and possibly the mother of attai. Parents: Sheshan.

genealogy.name: Ahohi
genealogy.bio: Ancestor of eleazar, one of king david's warriors.

genealogy.name: Aholibamah
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure (genesis 36). Parents: Anah, Anah's wife.

genealogy.name: Ahumai
genealogy.bio: Descendant of judah through his grandson reaiah.

genealogy.name: Ahuzzam
genealogy.bio: Ahuzzam was the son of ashhur and his wife naarah, from the tribe of judah.

genealogy.name: Ahuzzath
genealogy.bio: Friend and adviser of the king abimelech of gerar.

genealogy.name: Aiah
genealogy.bio: Father of the concubine of saul rizpah.

genealogy.name: Ajah
genealogy.bio: Male biblical figure in genesis 36:24, son of zibeon the hivite.

genealogy.name: Akan
genealogy.bio: Son of ezer and grandson of seir the horite.

genealogy.name: Akkub
genealogy.bio: Levite who assisted ezra in explaining the law to the people during the public reading.

genealogy.name: Alemeth
genealogy.bio: Son of becher and descendant of benjamin.

genealogy.name: Alexander
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure in mark 15:21, son of simon of cyrene. Parents: Simon of Cyrene.

genealogy.name: Alexander the Coppersmith

genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, a grandson of benjamin and one of the sons of bela. Parents: Bela.

genealogy.name: Vaizatha
genealogy.bio: Biblical human figure, son of haman. Parents: Haman, Zeresh.

genealogy.name: Vaniah
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure (book of ezra).

genealogy.name: Vashti
genealogy.bio: Character in the book of esther; queen of persia (book of esther). Parents: Belshazzar.

genealogy.name: Vophsi
genealogy.bio: In numbers 13:14, father of nahbi, from the tribe of naphtali.

genealogy.name: Whore of Babylon
genealogy.bio: New testament symbol.

genealogy.name: Widow of Zarephath
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure (1 kings 17).

genealogy.name: wife of Manoah
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure, mother of samson (judges).

genealogy.name: Wise woman of Abel
genealogy.bio: Biblical character (second book of samuel).

genealogy.name: Witch of Endor
genealogy.bio: Biblical character (first book of samuel).

genealogy.name: Woman of Tekoa
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure (second book of samuel).

genealogy.name: Yael
genealogy.bio: Woman in the book of judges, killeer of sisera (judges 4).

genealogy.name: Zaavan
genealogy.bio: Son of eser and grandson of seir the horite. Parents: Eser.

genealogy.name: Zabad
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, a son of ephraim, was killed by the men of gath. Parents: Ephraim.

genealogy.name: Zabbai
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, father of baruch, who repaired a section of jerusalem's wall.

genealogy.name: Zabdi
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, son of shimei.

genealogy.name: Zabdiel
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, the father of jashobeam, who was a commander in king david's army.

genealogy.name: Zabud
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure in 1 chronicles 2:36, son of nathan. Parents: Nathan.

genealogy.name: Zaccai
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, the ancestor of a family that returned to jerusalem after the babylonian exile.

genealogy.name: Zaccur
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, a merarite levite, one of the sons of jaaziah.

genealogy.name: Zadok II
genealogy.bio: Biblical character, father of shallum the high priest, different from zadok (first book of chronicles). Parents: Ahitub II.

genealogy.name: Zalaph
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, the father of hanun, who repaired a section of jerusalem's wall.

genealogy.name: Zalmon the Ahohite
genealogy.bio: One of king david's valiant men.

genealogy.name: Zalmunna
genealogy.bio: King of midian in the book of judges.

genealogy.name: Zaphoin
genealogy.bio: Leader of an edomite clan according to the septuagint.

genealogy.name: Zattu
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, one of the leaders of israel who sealed the covenant during nehemiah's time.

genealogy.name: Zattur
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, the ancestor of a family that returned to jerusalem after the babylonian exile.

genealogy.name: Zaza
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, a son of jonathan and a descendant of jerahmeel from the tribe of judah.

genealogy.name: Zebadiah
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, son of ishmael, was appointed as the leader in all matters concerning the king during jehoshaphat's reign.

genealogy.name: Zebah
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, a midianite king who, along with zalmunna, was defeated and killed by gideon during the time of the judges.

genealogy.name: Zebedee
genealogy.bio: Father of james and john, two disciples of jesus.
genealogy.children: Boanerges, John the Apostle, St. James the Elder

genealogy.name: Zebina
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, one of the israelites who had married foreign women during the time of ezr.

genealogy.name: Zebudah
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure (second book of kings). Parents: Pedaiah.

genealogy.name: Zebul
genealogy.bio: Mayor of shechem in abimilech's time per judges 9:28-41.

genealogy.name: Zechariah
genealogy.bio: Father of john the baptist; new testament biblical character.

genealogy.name: Zechariah ben Jehoiada
genealogy.bio: A figure in the hebrew bible described as a priest who was stoned to death by jehoash of judah. Parents: Jehoiada.

genealogy.name: Zecher
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, son of gabon and maacah, grandson of jeiel.

genealogy.name: Zedekiah
genealogy.bio: Uncle of Jeconiah. Rebelled against Babylon, leading to the destruction of Jerusalem.
genealogy.title: Last King of Judah

genealogy.name: Zedekiah
genealogy.bio: Biblical character and final king of judah. Parents: Josiah, Hamutal.

genealogy.name: Zelek the Ammonite
genealogy.bio: One of king david's valiant men.

genealogy.name: Zelophehad
genealogy.bio: In numbers 26:33, 27:1-11, son of hepher. Parents: Hepher.

genealogy.name: Zemirah
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, son of becher and grandson of benjamin. Parents: Becher.

genealogy.name: Zephaniah
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, father of josiah, was one of the men for whom the crown of josuah was to be placed in the temple as a memorial.

genealogy.name: Zephon
genealogy.bio: Son of eliphaz. Parents: Eliphaz.

genealogy.name: Zerah
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, from bozrah, father of jobab who was king of edom before israel had a king.

genealogy.name: Zerahiah
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure,  a descendant of pahath-moab and father of eliehoenai who led 200 men in the return to jerusalem with ezr.

genealogy.name: Zeresh
genealogy.bio: Wife of haman in the book of esther; advises him to prepare gallows to hang mordecai (book of esther).

genealogy.name: Zereth
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, son of ashhur and helah, a descendant of judah. Parents: Ashhur.

genealogy.name: Zeri
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, son of jeduthun, who prophesied with a lyre at the temple. Parents: Jeduthun.

genealogy.name: Zerubbabel
genealogy.bio: Led the first group of exiles back to Jerusalem and laid the foundation for the Second Temple.
genealogy.title: Governor of Judah

genealogy.name: Zeruiah
genealogy.bio: Biblical character (second book of samuel). Parents: Jesse, Nitzevet.

genealogy.name: Zetham
genealogy.bio: Son of laadan and a gershonite levite during the time of david.

genealogy.name: Zethan
genealogy.bio: Son of bilhan and a descendant of benjamin.

genealogy.name: Zethar
genealogy.bio: In esther 1:10, court official of king xerxes.

genealogy.name: Zia
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure,  a gadite clan leader during the reigns of jotham, king of judah, and jeroboam, king of israel.

genealogy.name: Ziba
genealogy.bio: In 2 samuel 9:2-12.

genealogy.name: Zibeon
genealogy.bio: Biblical character. Parents: Seir, mother of Zibeon.

genealogy.name: Zibia
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure,  son of shaharaim, a benjamite, born to his wife hodesh in the country of moab.

genealogy.name: Zibiah
genealogy.bio: Biblical character.

genealogy.name: Zichri
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure,  father of amasiah, (a commander of 200,000 soldiers who volunteered to serve king jehoshaphat of judah.).

genealogy.name: Zidon
genealogy.bio: Biblical character (genesis 10). Parents: Canaan.

genealogy.name: Ziha
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, head of a family of temple servants.

genealogy.name: Zillah
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure (genesis 4).

genealogy.name: Zillethai
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, leader from the tribe of manasseh, one of the men who joined david at ziklag.

genealogy.name: Zilpa
genealogy.bio: Biblical character. Parents: http://www.wikidata.org/.well-known/genid/c9b0f4b3496c4398577604cbf40a311c, http://www.wikidata.org/.well-known/genid/e172c1e79f6461cc01eff9245db23f3f.

genealogy.name: Zilpah
genealogy.bio: Given to Jacob by Leah to bear children on her behalf.
genealogy.title: Maidservant of Leah

genealogy.name: Zimmah
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, a levite and descendant of gershom, son of shimei and the father of ethan.

genealogy.name: Zimri
genealogy.bio: Prince of the tribe of simeon (book of numbers).

genealogy.name: Ziph
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure,  descendant of judah, was one of the sons of jehallelel.

genealogy.name: Ziphah
genealogy.bio: Biblical character, son of jehallelel (books of chronicles).

genealogy.name: Ziphion
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure. Parents: Gad.

genealogy.name: Zippor
genealogy.bio: Father of the king of moab balak.

genealogy.name: Zipporah
genealogy.bio: Biblical character, wife of moses. Parents: Jethro.

genealogy.name: Ziza
genealogy.bio: A descendant of simeon, was the son of shiphi.

genealogy.name: Zizah
genealogy.bio: One of the sons of shimei, a descendant of jacob.

genealogy.name: Zobebah
genealogy.bio: Biblical figure, one of the sons of koz, a descendant of judah.

genealogy.name: Zohar
genealogy.bio: Father of ephron the hittite, from whom abraham purchased the cave of machpelah as a burial site.

genealogy.name: Zophar
genealogy.bio: One of job's comforters.

genealogy.name: Zuar
genealogy.bio: Father of nethanel (tribe of issachar).

genealogy.name: Zulaikha
genealogy.reference: Jewish Encyclopedia of Brockhaus and Efron
genealogy.bio: Character from the old testament (genesis 39).

genealogy.name: Zuph the Ephrathite
genealogy.bio: Human biblical figure in 1 chronicles 6:35, father of toah or tohu or nathan, an descendant of ephraim, son of elkanah. Parents: Elkanah.

genealogy.name: Zur
genealogy.bio: Midianite leader defeated by israel during the conquest of canaan whose daughter was involved with an israelite, resulting in a plague on israel.

genealogy.name: Zuriel
genealogy.bio: Son of abihail ; levite and head of the ancestral house of the clans of merari.

genealogy.name: Zurishaddai
genealogy.bio: In numbers 1:6 & 2:12, father of shelumiel.

counseling.steps: Look to the Lord as your stronghold and refuge in times of trouble (Psalm 9:9). Speak up for yourself and others who are oppressed, ensuring the rights of the destitute are protected (Proverbs 31:8). Seek safety and support from those who can provide a stronghold against the misuse of power (Psalm 9:9).
counseling.title: Abuse + Oppression
counseling.scriptures: Psalm 9:9: The LORD is a stronghold for the oppressed, a stronghold in times of trouble. Proverbs 31:8: Open your mouth for the mute, for the rights of all who are destitute.
counseling.definition: Abuse and oppression involve the misuse of power to harm or silence others, which is addressed by looking to the Lord as a stronghold for the oppressed and advocating for the rights of the destitute.

Addiction is the state of being dominated by a substance or behavior, which is addressed by relying on the grace of God to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions while seeking true provision in Him.
counseling.title: Addiction + Substance
counseling.steps: Acknowledge that you must not be dominated by any substance or behavior (1 Corinthians 6:12). Rely on the grace of God to train you to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions (Titus 2:11-12). Seek the escape and true provision that only God can provide rather than turning to a functional savior (Titus 2:12).
counseling.scriptures: 1 Corinthians 6:12: I will not be dominated by anything. Titus 2:11-12: The grace of God... training us to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions.

counseling.definition: Aging is the natural process of physical decline and mortality, which is met by the daily renewal of the inner self through the gospel and the pursuit of a heart of wisdom by recognizing the brevity of life.
counseling.title: Aging + Mortality
counseling.steps: Do not lose heart as your physical body declines, but focus on the daily renewal of your inner self (2 Corinthians 4:16). Seek a heart of wisdom by numbering your days and recognizing the brevity of life (Psalm 90:12). Navigate the approach of death by placing your hope in the gospel (2 Corinthians 4:16).
counseling.scriptures: 2 Corinthians 4:16: So we do not lose heart. Though our outer self is wasting away, our inner self is being renewed day by day. Psalm 90:12: So teach us to number our days that we may get a heart of wisdom.

counseling.steps: Bring your anxieties to God through prayer and supplication with thanksgiving (Philippians 4:6). Cast all your anxieties on Him because He cares for you (1 Peter 5:7). Trust in God's sovereign care rather than attempting to control the future (Philippians 4:7).
counseling.title: Anger + Bitterness
counseling.scriptures: James 1:19-20: Let every person be quick to hear, slow to speak, slow to anger; for the anger of man does not produce the righteousness of God. Ephesians 4:26: Be angry and do not sin; do not let the sun go down on your anger.
counseling.definition: Anger is a sinful passion that fails to produce God's righteousness, which is addressed by being quick to hear, slow to speak, and resolving wrath before the day ends to avoid sin.
Anxiety is the state of being fearful or worried about the future, which is addressed by bringing requests to God through prayer and casting all cares upon Him because of His sovereign concern.
counseling.title: Anxiety + Fear
counseling.steps: Bring your anxieties to God through prayer and supplication with thanksgiving (Philippians 4:6). Cast all your anxieties on Him because He cares for you (1 Peter 5:7). Trust in God's sovereign care rather than attempting to control the future (Philippians 4:7).
counseling.scriptures: Philippians 4:6-7: Do not be anxious about anything, but in everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God. 1 Peter 5:7: Casting all your anxieties on him, because he cares for you.

counseling.steps: Seek the grace of God to ensure that no root of bitterness springs up and causes trouble (Hebrews 12:15). Actively put away all bitterness, wrath, anger, clamor, and slander from your life (Ephesians 4:31).
counseling.title: Bitterness
counseling.scriptures: Hebrews 12:15: See to it that no one fails to obtain the grace of God; that no 'root of bitterness' springs up and causes trouble. Ephesians 4:31: Let all bitterness and wrath and anger and clamor and slander be put away from you.
counseling.definition: Bitterness is a deep-seated resentment or "root" that springs up when one fails to obtain the grace of God, leading to trouble and defilement, and must be intentionally put away in favor of a life free from wrath and slander.

counseling.steps: Seek God's presence to receive the rest He has promised (Exodus 33:14). Come to Jesus when you are burdened and weary from overwork to find relief and rest (Matthew 11:28). Avoid spiritual and physical depletion by prioritizing Sabbath rest and dwelling in God's presence (Exodus 33:14).
counseling.title: Burnout + Exhaustion
counseling.scriptures: Exodus 33:14: And he said, 'My presence will go with you, and I will give you rest.' Matthew 11:28: Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.
counseling.definition: Burnout is a state of physical and spiritual exhaustion resulting from heavy labor and burdens, which is resolved by seeking rest in the presence of God and the person of Jesus Christ.

counseling.steps: If a brother sins against you, go and tell him his fault privately between the two of you alone (Matthew 18:15). If anyone is caught in a transgression, those who are spiritual should seek to restore them in a spirit of gentleness (Galatians 6:1). Approach the process as a loving and restorative way to address unrepentant sin within the covenant community.
counseling.title: Church Discipline
counseling.scriptures: Matthew 18:15: If your brother sins against you, go and tell him his fault, between you and him alone. Galatians 6:1: Brothers, if anyone is caught in any transgression, you who are spiritual should restore him in a spirit of gentleness.
counseling.definition: Church discipline is the restorative process of addressing sin within the covenant community by privately confronting a brother and seeking to restore them in a spirit of gentleness.
Conflict resolution is the pursuit of peaceable living and restoration through private, direct communication and the gracious navigation of disagreements to maintain unity.
counseling.title: Conflict Resolution
counseling.scriptures: Romans 12:18: If possible, so far as it depends on you, live peaceably with all. Matthew 18:15: If your brother sins against you, go and tell him his fault, between you and him alone.
counseling.steps: Endeavor to live peaceably with everyone as much as it depends on you (Romans 12:18). If someone sins against you, address the fault privately and directly with them to seek restoration (Matthew 18:15). Navigate disagreements with grace while seeking unity and truthfully addressing wrongs.

Pursue godliness along with contentment, recognizing it as great gain (1 Timothy 6:6). Learn the secret of being content in every situation, whether in abundance or in need, by trusting in God's provision (Philippians 4:12). Find restful trust in God's providence regardless of your external circumstances (1 Timothy 6:6, Philippians 4:12).
counseling.title: Contentment
counseling.scriptures: 1 Timothy 6:6: But godliness with contentment is great gain. Philippians 4:12: I have learned the secret of facing plenty and hunger, abundance and need.
counseling.definition: Contentment is the inner state of restful trust in God's providence and provision, characterized by the pursuit of godliness and the ability to face any external circumstance with peace.

counseling.steps: Trust in the LORD with all your heart and do not lean on your own understanding (Proverbs 3:5). Ask God for wisdom when you lack it, and He will give it to you (James 1:5). Acknowledge Him in all your ways so that He will make your paths straight (Proverbs 3:6).
counseling.title: Decision + Wisdom
counseling.scriptures: Proverbs 3:5-6: Trust in the LORD with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding. James 1:5: If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God.
counseling.definition: Biblical decision-making is the process of seeking God's guidance and wisdom through prayer, the study of Scripture, and godly counsel to make choices that align with His will and bring Him glory. It involves trusting in the Lord's understanding rather than one's own, acknowledging Him in all ways, and relying on the Holy Spirit to provide discernment and direction for one's paths.
Depression and despair involve a state of profound sadness and a crushed spirit, which is addressed by placing one's hope in God and finding comfort in His nearness to the brokenhearted.
counseling.title: Depression + Despair
counseling.steps: Address your profound sadness and hopelessness by placing your hope in God, knowing you will praise Him again (Psalm 42:5). Take comfort in the truth that the LORD is near to the brokenhearted and saves those with a crushed spirit (Psalm 34:18).
counseling.scriptures: Psalm 42:5: Why are you cast down, O my soul... Hope in God; for I shall again praise him, my salvation and my God. Psalm 34:18: The LORD is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit.

counseling.definition: Doubt is the internal struggle to believe God's promises and character, which is addressed by acknowledging one's unbelief through prayer and choosing to remember the Lord's past deeds and faithfulness.
counseling.steps: Acknowledge your struggle to believe God's promises and pray for help with your unbelief (Mark 9:24). Actively choose to remember and meditate on the deeds and past faithfulness of the Lord (Psalm 77:11).
counseling.title: Doubt + Faith
counseling.scriptures: Mark 9:24: I believe; help my unbelief! Psalm 77:11: I will remember the deeds of the LORD.

counseling.definition: Envy is a resentful longing for another's blessings or success that stems from a dissatisfied heart and a failure to trust God's provision. It is a destructive work of the flesh that rots the bones and leads to disorder, addressed by learning the secret of contentment and resting in God's goodness.
counseling.title: Envy + Discontent
counseling.steps: Recognize that resenting others' blessings is destructive to your own well-being (Proverbs 14:30). Actively learn to be content in whatever situation you are in by trusting God's provision (Philippians 4:11). Reject the belief that your current blessings are insufficient and choose to rest in God's goodness (Philippians 4:11).
counseling.scriptures: Proverbs 14:30: Envy makes the bones rot. Philippians 4:11: I have learned in whatever situation I am to be content.

counseling.steps: Trust in the Lord for safety rather than being trapped by the fear of others' opinions (Proverbs 29:25). Prioritize being a servant of Christ over trying to please people (Galatians 1:10). Seek the approval and reverence of God above the approval of man (Galatians 1:10).
counseling.title: Fear of Man
counseling.scriptures: Proverbs 29:25: The fear of man lays a snare, but whoever trusts in the LORD is safe. Galatians 1:10: If I were still trying to please man, I would not be a servant of Christ.
counseling.definition: The fear of man is a heart issue where one reveres, obeys, and seeks the approval of people more than God, creating a dangerous snare that leads to hypocrisy and compromises gospel fidelity. It is the state of prioritizing human opinion and the fear of rejection over the sovereign authority and reverence of the Lord.

Finances and greed involve the idolatrous pursuit of wealth and security, which is addressed by choosing to serve God alone rather than money and recognizing that the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil.
counseling.steps: Handle money as a steward rather than an owner to address the idolatry of wealth and security. Guard against the love of money, as it is a root of all kinds of evil (1 Timothy 6:10). Choose to serve God alone, acknowledging that you cannot serve both God and money (Matthew 6:24).
counseling.title: Finances + Greed
counseling.scriptures: 1 Timothy 6:10: For the love of money is a root of all kinds of evils. Matthew 6:24: You cannot serve God and money.

counseling.definition: Forgiveness is the act of releasing others from the debt of their offenses, modeled after God's limitlessly merciful and sacrificial pardon of believers through Jesus Christ.
counseling.title: Forgiveness
counseling.scriptures: Ephesians 4:32: Forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you. Matthew 18:21-22: I do not say to you seven times, but seventy-seven times.
counseling.steps: Forgive one another, modeling your response after the way God in Christ forgave you (Ephesians 4:32). Extend forgiveness repeatedly and without limit, refusing to keep count of offenses (Matthew 18:21-22). Release all offenses and pursue peace based on the reality of the debt God has already cleared for you (Ephesians 4:32).

Avoid overindulgence in food or drink by being mindful of your physical appetites (Proverbs 23:20-21). Ensure that whether you eat or drink, you do everything with the intention of bringing glory to God (1 Corinthians 10:31). Seek comfort in the Creator rather than the creation by relying on Him to satisfy your desires (1 Corinthians 10:31).
counseling.title: Gluttony + Appetite
counseling.scriptures: Proverbs 23:20-21: Be not among drunkards or among gluttonous eaters of meat. 1 Corinthians 10:31: So, whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God.
counseling.definition: Gluttony is the sin of overindulgence and a lack of self-control regarding physical appetites, where one's desire for food or material pleasure becomes an idol that takes priority over devotion to God. It is characterized by consuming to excess or with greedy eagerness, ultimately reflecting a heart that seeks satisfaction in the creation rather than the Creator.

counseling.steps: Replace corrupting talk with words that are good for building up others (Ephesians 4:29). Refrain from whispering or spreading unnecessary information to avoid separating close friends (Proverbs 16:28). Use your speech to protect the reputation of others rather than tearing them down (Ephesians 4:29).
counseling.title: Gossip + Slander
counseling.scriptures: Ephesians 4:29: Let no corrupting talk come out of your mouths, but only such as is good for building up. Proverbs 16:28: A whisperer separates close friends.
counseling.definition: Gossip and slander involve the sinful use of speech to bear bad news behind someone's back out of a corrupt heart, characterized by breaking confidentiality or spreading corrupting talk that separates close friends rather than building others up.

counseling.steps: Grieve with hope, knowing that in Christ, sorrow is not without a future (1 Thessalonians 4:13). Draw near to the Lord in your sorrow, trusting that He is close to those who are brokenhearted (Psalm 34:18).
counseling.title: Grief + Loss
counseling.scriptures: 1 Thessalonians 4:13: That you may not grieve as others do who have no hope. Psalm 34:18: The LORD is near to the brokenhearted.
counseling.definition: Grief is the experience of sorrow and brokenheartedness that, for the believer, is marked by hope in Christ and the comfort of God’s presence rather than the despair of those without a future.

counseling.steps: Examine your heart for any discrepancy between your outward appearance and inward reality (Matthew 23:27-28). Practice the truth by walking in the light and ensuring your fellowship with God is genuine (1 John 1:6). Align your behavior with your claimed moral standards and beliefs to ensure integrity (Matthew 23:28).
counseling.title: Hypocrisy
counseling.scriptures: Matthew 23:27-28: You also outwardly appear righteous to others, but within you are full of hypocrisy and lawlessness. 1 John 1:6: If we say we have fellowship with him while we walk in darkness, we lie and do not practice the truth.
counseling.definition: Hypocrisy is the state of appearing righteous to others while harboring lawlessness and darkness within, characterized by a discrepancy between outward religious performance and the true condition of one's heart. It is addressed by walking in the light and ensuring that one's fellowship with God and moral standards are genuine rather than a mere public pretense.

counseling.steps: Recognize that as a believer, you are a new creation in Christ where the old has passed away and the new has come (2 Corinthians 5:17). Live with the understanding that you have been crucified with Christ and it is no longer you who live, but Christ who lives in you (Galatians 2:20). Base your foundational worth and identity on your union with Jesus rather than your own performance or the opinions of others (Galatians 2:20).
counseling.title: Identity in Christ
counseling.scriptures: 2 Corinthians 5:17: Therefore, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come. Galatians 2:20: I have been crucified with Christ. It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me.
counseling.definition: Identity in Christ is the state of being a new creation through union with Jesus, where one’s old life is replaced by Christ living within them, providing a foundational worth based on His work rather than personal performance.
Idolatry is the act of placing anything above the living God in one's love, trust, or obedience, which is addressed by keeping oneself from idols and having no other gods before Him.
counseling.steps: Keep yourself from idols by identifying and removing anything you love, trust, or obey more than the living God (1 John 5:21). Commit to having no other gods before Him, ensuring He remains the sole object of your worship and priority (Exodus 20:3).
counseling.title: Idolatry + Worship
counseling.scriptures: 1 John 5:21: Little children, keep yourselves from idols. Exodus 20:3: You shall have no other gods before me.

counseling.definition: Injustice and oppression represent the violation of God's standards of righteousness through the unfair treatment of others, particularly the vulnerable. It is addressed by actively seeking what is good, correcting oppression, and advocating for the rights of the fatherless and the widow as an act of obedience to the Lord.
counseling.steps: Actively seek to do what is good and right according to God's standards (Micah 6:8). Commit to seeking justice and correcting oppression wherever it is found (Isaiah 1:17). Show love and kindness to others while advocating for the rights of the fatherless and the widow (Micah 6:8, Isaiah 1:17).
counseling.title: Injustice + Oppression
counseling.scriptures: Micah 6:8: He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does the LORD require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness... Isaiah 1:17: Learn to do good; seek justice, correct oppression; bring justice to the fatherless, plead the widow's cause.

counseling.definition: Jealousy is a destructive and sinful passion characterized by selfish ambition and resentment toward the advantages or blessings of others, which leads to disorder and every vile practice. It is regarded as a powerful force more overwhelming than wrath or anger, requiring a heart turned toward God's peace and order to overcome.
counseling.title: Jealousy
counseling.steps: Recognize that where jealousy and selfish ambition exist, there will be disorder and every vile practice (James 3:16). Acknowledge the destructive power of jealousy, which can be more overwhelming than wrath or anger (Proverbs 27:4). Turn from resentful longing and suspicious fear toward a pursuit of peace and godly order (James 3:16).
counseling.scriptures: James 3:16: For where jealousy and selfish ambition exist, there will be disorder and every vile practice. Proverbs 27:4: Wrath is cruel, anger is overwhelming, but who can stand before jealousy?

counseling.steps: Recognize that you are perfected by the Spirit rather than your own flesh or human effort (Galatians 3:3). Reject the reliance on man-made regulations and human performance for your sanctification (Colossians 2:20-22). Trust fully in God's grace for your justification instead of religious rules (Colossians 2:20).
counseling.title: Legalism
counseling.scriptures: Galatians 3:3: Are you so foolish? Having begun by the Spirit, are you now being perfected by the flesh? Colossians 2:20-22: If with Christ you died to the elemental spirits of the world, why... do you submit to regulations?
counseling.definition: Legalism is the attempt to earn God's favor or secure righteousness through human effort, religious rituals, or the strict adherence to man-made rules and traditions rather than relying on the finished work of Christ and the grace of the Holy Spirit.

Loneliness and isolation is the state of feeling solitary or forsaken, which is addressed by trusting in God's promise of His constant presence and His design for believers to find a home in His community.
counseling.title: Loneliness + Isolation
counseling.steps: Trust in God's promise that He will never leave you nor forsake you (Hebrews 13:5). Find comfort in knowing that God settles the solitary in a home and designed you for communion with Him and His people (Psalm 68:6).
counseling.scriptures: Psalm 68:6: God settles the solitary in a home. Hebrews 13:5: He has said, 'I will never leave you nor forsake you.'

counseling.steps: Put away all falsehood and intentional misleading of others (Ephesians 4:25). Commit to speaking the truth honestly with your neighbor (Ephesians 4:25). Strive to act faithfully in your communication, seeking to be a delight to the Lord (Proverbs 12:22).
counseling.title: Lying + Deceit
counseling.scriptures: Proverbs 12:22: Lying lips are an abomination to the LORD, but those who act faithfully are his delight. Ephesians 4:25: Therefore, having put away falsehood, let each one of you speak the truth with his neighbor.
counseling.definition: Lying and deceit is the intentional misleading or beguiling of others through falsehood or guile, an act that is detestable to the Lord but is overcome by putting away falsehood and speaking truthfully to one's neighbor.
Marriage is a lifelong covenant that mirrors the relationship between Christ and the church, fulfilled through a husband's sacrificial love and a wife's respect.
counseling.steps: Husbands should love their wives sacrificially, just as Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her (Ephesians 5:25). Each husband must love his wife as himself, and each wife must ensure that she respects her husband (Ephesians 5:33). Maintain the marriage as a lifelong covenant that displays the mystery of Christ's love for His church through mutual sacrificial love and respect (Ephesians 5:25, 33).
counseling.title: Marriage + Covenant
counseling.scriptures: Ephesians 5:25: Husbands, love your wives, as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her. Ephesians 5:33: Let each one of you love his wife as himself, and let the wife see that she respects her husband.

counseling.definition: Parenting is the stewardship of children by bringing them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord while diligently teaching them His Word and avoiding provocation to anger.
counseling.steps:
- Train and instruct your children in the ways of the Lord to avoid provoking them to anger (Ephesians 6:4)
  - Diligently teach God's Word to your children throughout the day, whether at home or traveling (Deuteronomy 6:7)
  - Model the discipline and instruction of the Lord as a steward of His grace (Ephesians 6:4)
counseling.title: Parenting + Family
counseling.scriptures: Ephesians 6:4: Fathers, do not provoke your children to anger, but bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord. Deuteronomy 6:7: You shall teach them diligently to your children, and shall talk of them when you sit in your house...

counseling.definition: Pastoral care is the ministry of shepherding God's flock by providing spiritual, emotional, and practical support, modeled after the Good Shepherd who seeks the lost and cares for His sheep. It involves leading through godly example, offering tender leadership during crises, and drawing individuals toward the mercy and restorative grace of God.
counseling.steps: Shepherd the flock of God by being a godly example to those under your care (1 Peter 5:2-3). Seek the lost and bring back the strayed, reflecting God's own heart as the Good Shepherd (Ezekiel 34:15-16). Provide tender and attentive leadership, especially during times of crisis (1 Peter 5:2).
counseling.title: Pastoral Care Needs
counseling.scriptures: 1 Peter 5:2-3: Shepherd the flock of God that is among you... being examples to the flock. Ezekiel 34:15-16: I myself will be the shepherd of my sheep... I will seek the lost, and I will bring back the strayed...

counseling.steps: Be patient and endure until the coming of the Lord (James 5:7-8). Wait for the Lord with strength and a courageous heart (Psalm 27:14). Trust quietly in God's perfect timing during delays, difficulties, or suffering (James 5:8).
counseling.title: Patience + Waiting
counseling.scriptures: James 5:7-8: Be patient, therefore, brothers, until the coming of the Lord. Psalm 27:14: Wait for the LORD; be strong, and let your heart take courage; wait for the LORD!
counseling.definition: Patience is the God-given restraint and endurance to wait for the Lord's timing with a strong and courageous heart, trusting in His sovereignty and promises even during delays, difficulties, or suffering.

counseling.steps: Acknowledge that no one on earth is perfectly righteous or without sin (Ecclesiastes 7:20). Rely on God's grace as sufficient for your weaknesses, allowing His power to be made perfect in your imperfections (2 Corinthians 12:9). Shift your focus from flawless performance to trusting in God's strength (2 Corinthians 12:9).
counseling.title: Perfectionism
counseling.scriptures: Ecclesiastes 7:20: Surely there is not a righteous man on earth who does good and never sins. 2 Corinthians 12:9: But he said to me, 'My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.'
counseling.definition: Perfectionism is the unrealistic pursuit of flawless performance and the denial of human fallibility, which is addressed by relying on God's sufficient grace and finding strength in His power made perfect through our weaknesses.
Pride is the state of having a haughty spirit that leads to destruction, which is addressed by humbling oneself before God to receive His grace instead of His opposition.
counseling.steps: Recognize that pride leads to destruction and a haughty spirit precedes a fall (Proverbs 16:18). Humble yourself before God to receive His grace, as He opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble (James 4:6).
counseling.title: Pride + Arrogance
counseling.scriptures: Proverbs 16:18: Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall. James 4:6: God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.

counseling.steps: Abstain from sexual immorality, recognizing that it is the will of God for your life (1 Thessalonians 4:3-4). Guard your heart and mind by refusing to look at others with lustful intent (Matthew 5:28). Seek to honor God's design for intimacy by exercising self-control over your own body (1 Thessalonians 4:4).
counseling.title: Purity + Lust
counseling.scriptures: 1 Thessalonians 4:3-4: This is the will of God... that you abstain from sexual immorality. Matthew 5:28: Everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart.
counseling.definition: Purity is the commitment to abstain from sexual immorality and lustful intent in accordance with God's will, honoring His design for intimacy through self-control of the heart and body.

Rebellion is the willful defiance and opposition to God's ordained authority and standards, originating in a prideful heart that seeks self-rule over submission. It is a serious sin, biblically equated with witchcraft and idolatry, that disrupts the divine order established in the home, church, and society.
counseling.title: Rebellion + Authority
counseling.scriptures: Romans 13:1: Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God. Hebrews 13:17: Obey your leaders and submit to them, for they are keeping watch over your souls.
counseling.steps: Be subject to governing authorities, recognizing that there is no authority except from God (Romans 13:1). Obey your leaders and submit to them, as they are keeping watch over your souls (Hebrews 13:17).

counseling.definition: Rejection is the experience of being despised or forsaken by others, which is addressed by finding comfort in Christ's shared suffering and trusting in the Lord's promise to receive and care for those who are abandoned.
counseling.steps: Recognize that Jesus understands your pain, as He was also despised and rejected by men (Isaiah 53:3). Trust that the Lord will take you in and care for you, even if you are forsaken by those closest to you (Psalm 27:10).
counseling.title: Rejection
counseling.scriptures: Isaiah 53:3: He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief. Psalm 27:10: For my father and my mother have forsaken me, but the LORD will take me in.

counseling.steps: Recognize that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit and belongs to God (1 Corinthians 6:19). Meditate on the truth that you are fearfully and wonderfully made by the Creator (Psalm 139:14). Seek to honor God with your physical body rather than using it as a means to cope with emotional pain (1 Corinthians 6:20).
counseling.title: Self-Harm
counseling.scriptures: 1 Corinthians 6:19: Or do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you...? Psalm 139:14: I praise you, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made.
counseling.definition: Self-harm is a destructive coping mechanism where an individual deliberately inflicts physical injury upon their body—which is the temple of the Holy Spirit—as a misguided attempt to manage intense emotional pain, shame, or a perceived need for self-punishment rather than seeking relief and restoration through the care of God.

Shame is the painful emotion of disgrace or humiliation regarding one's identity as a person, while guilt is the objective moral or legal state of deserving punishment for wrongdoing, both of which are fully addressed by the cleansing and non-condemning grace of Jesus Christ.
counseling.steps: Embrace the truth that there is no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus to overcome the feeling of being flawed (Romans 8:1). Confess your sins to God, trusting that He is faithful and just to forgive and cleanse you (1 John 1:9). Use the power of the Gospel to address both the objective reality of guilt and the subjective weight of shame (Romans 8:1, 1 John 1:9).
counseling.title: Shame + Guilt
counseling.scriptures: Romans 8:1: There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus. 1 John 1:9: If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins.

counseling.steps: Embrace singleness as an opportunity to be free from worldly anxieties and to focus your attention on the things of the Lord (1 Corinthians 7:32). Trust in God's promise that he provides a place and an everlasting name to those who keep His covenants, regardless of their marital status (Isaiah 56:4-5). Use this season for undivided devotion to the Lord (1 Corinthians 7:32).
counseling.title: Singleness
counseling.scriptures: 1 Corinthians 7:32: I want you to be free from anxieties. The unmarried man is anxious about the things of the Lord. Isaiah 56:4-5: To the eunuchs who keep my Sabbaths... I will give them an everlasting name.
counseling.definition: Singleness is a gift from God characterized by the state of being unmarried, which provides a unique opportunity for undivided devotion to the Lord and freedom from the anxieties of worldly affairs. It is a valid and valuable calling that allows a person to focus their attention on how to please God and serve His kingdom without the distractions and divided interests inherent in marriage.

counseling.steps: Consider the example of the ant to gain wisdom and avoid laziness (Proverbs 6:6). Work heartily in everything you do, as for the Lord rather than for men (Colossians 3:23). Faithfully steward your time and gifts for God to prevent chaos and missed opportunities (Proverbs 6:6).
counseling.title: Sloth + Laziness
counseling.scriptures: Proverbs 6:6: Go to the ant, O sluggard; consider her ways, and be wise. Colossians 3:23: Work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men.
counseling.definition: Sloth is a state of spiritual apathy and physical idleness characterized by a lack of diligence and a failure to steward God-given responsibilities, often manifesting as a "defective love" that prioritizes personal ease over the commands and priorities of God. It is addressed by observing the industriousness of God's creation and committing to work heartily for the Lord rather than for human approval.

Spiritual abuse is the misuse of power and authority by leaders who rule with force, harshness, or manipulation instead of shepherding God's people with a willing spirit and being Christ-like examples.
counseling.steps: Recognize the misuse of power and the failure of leaders to strengthen or heal the weak (Ezekiel 34:2-4). Seek spiritual leaders who shepherd the flock with a willing spirit rather than through domineering or harsh rule (1 Peter 5:2-3). Look for examples of leadership that reflect the character of Christ rather than manipulation or control (1 Peter 5:3).
counseling.title: Spiritual Abuse
counseling.scriptures: Ezekiel 34:2-4: The weak you have not strengthened, the sick you have not healed... but with force and harshness you have ruled them. 1 Peter 5:2-3: Shepherd the flock of God... not domineering over those in your charge, but being examples to the flock.

counseling.definition: Suffering and pain in a fallen world are tools used by God to produce endurance, character, and hope, while serving as a reminder that His grace is sufficient and His power is made perfect in human weakness.
counseling.title: Suffering + Pain
counseling.scriptures: Romans 5:3-4: Suffering produces endurance, and endurance produces character. 2 Corinthians 12:9: My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.
counseling.steps: Recognize that suffering produces endurance, character, and hope (Romans 5:3-4). Rely on God's grace as your sufficient source of strength, knowing His power is made perfect in your weakness (2 Corinthians 12:9). Trust in God's sovereignty to use pain to refine your faith.

counseling.definition: Suicide and despair involve reaching a point of overwhelming pain and a desire to give up on life, which is addressed by honestly crying out to the Lord for help and trusting in His promise to hear and deliver the righteous from their troubles.
counseling.steps: Cry out to the Lord for help in your trouble, knowing He hears and delivers the righteous (Psalm 34:17). Be honest with God about your despair and desire to give up, just as Elijah expressed his desire to die (1 Kings 19:4). Trust that the Lord is near to deliver you from overwhelming pain (Psalm 34:17).
counseling.title: Suicide + Despair
counseling.scriptures: Psalm 34:17: When the righteous cry for help, the LORD hears and delivers them out of all their troubles. 1 Kings 19:4: But he himself went a day's journey into the wilderness... and asked that he might die.

counseling.definition: Trauma is the experience of deep emotional or spiritual wounding that is addressed by drawing near to the Lord for restoration and healing, trusting in His presence and care for the brokenhearted.
counseling.title: Trauma + Deep Wounds
counseling.steps: Trust in the Lord for restoration, knowing that He heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds (Psalm 147:3). Draw near to God in your pain, believing that He is close to the brokenhearted and saves those with a crushed spirit (Psalm 34:18).
counseling.scriptures: Psalm 147:3: He heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds. Psalm 34:18: The LORD is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit.

counseling.steps: Trust in the Lord with all your heart and do not lean on your own understanding (Proverbs 3:5). Rely on God's character, promises, and sovereignty by keeping your mind stayed on Him (Isaiah 26:3). Seek the perfect peace that comes from trusting in the Lord in every circumstance (Isaiah 26:3).
counseling.title: Trusting God
counseling.scriptures: Proverbs 3:5-6: Trust in the LORD with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding. Isaiah 26:3: You keep him in perfect peace whose mind is stayed on you, because he trusts in you.
counseling.definition: Trusting God is the deliberate choice to place complete confidence in His character, promises, and sovereignty, rather than relying on one's own limited understanding, resulting in a state of perfect peace.

Vanity is the sinful preoccupation with outward appearance and self-importance that prioritizes the temporal over the eternal, which is addressed by shifting focus from external adornment to the condition of the heart and one's identity in Christ.
counseling.steps: Prioritize the condition of your heart over outward appearance, as God looks at the heart rather than external beauty (1 Samuel 16:7). Focus on cultivating the "hidden person of the heart" through a gentle and quiet spirit rather than relying on external adornment (1 Peter 3:3-4).
counseling.scriptures: 1 Samuel 16:7: For the LORD sees not as man sees: man looks on the outward appearance, but the LORD looks on the heart. 1 Peter 3:3-4: Do not let your adorning be external... but let your adorning be the hidden person of the heart.
counseling.title: Vanity + Body Image

counseling.steps: Fulfill your promises to God without delay (Ecclesiastes 5:4). Practice integrity in your speech by letting your "yes" be "yes" and your "no" be "no" (Matthew 5:37). Follow the biblical mandate to be faithful in all commitments made to God and others (Ecclesiastes 5:4, Matthew 5:37).
counseling.title: Vows + Commitments
counseling.scriptures: Ecclesiastes 5:4: When you vow a vow to God, do not delay paying it, for he has no pleasure in fools. Matthew 5:37: Let what you say be simply 'Yes' or 'No'; anything more than this comes from evil.
counseling.definition: Vows and commitments are solemn promises made to God or others that reflect personal integrity and devotion, requiring faithful fulfillment without delay. Grounded in the character of a promise-keeping God, these binding obligations transcend fluctuating feelings and demand a simple, honest "yes" or "no" in all speech.

Work is the physical or mental effort established by God as a means to steward creation and glorify Him through diligent labor, primarily serving as an act of worship rather than a mere means of survival. While the Fall introduced toil and difficulty, work remains a purposeful mandate where believers serve the Lord heartily, seeking to benefit others and reflect God's own creative and sustaining nature.
counseling.steps: Work heartily in all your tasks, doing them for the Lord rather than for human approval (Colossians 3:23). Commit to diligent labor in your vocation, recognizing that there is profit in all toil (Proverbs 14:23). Avoid the extremes of idleness and mere talk by engaging in productive and honorable daily labor (Proverbs 14:23).
counseling.scriptures: Colossians 3:23: Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men. Proverbs 14:23: In all toil there is profit, but mere talk tends only to poverty.
counseling.title: Work + Vocation

counseling.steps: Do not love the world or the things in the world, recognizing that love for the world is incompatible with love for the Father (1 John 2:15). Refuse to be conformed to the values and priorities of this world (Romans 12:2). Be transformed by the renewal of your mind to discern and follow God's will (Romans 12:2).
counseling.title: Worldliness
counseling.scriptures: 1 John 2:15: Do not love the world or the things in the world. If anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him. Romans 12:2: Do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewal of your mind.
counseling.definition: Worldliness is the pursuit of desires and values that prioritize the world over the Father, requiring a transformation of the mind to resist conformity and discern God's will.

devotionals.question: What "door" am I standing too close to right now that I need to walk away from?
devotionals.reflection: Wisdom doesn't flirt with temptation to see how close it can get. True strength is found in staying as far away from the "door" of compromise as possible.
devotionals.scripture: Keep your way far from her, and do not go near the door of her house... — Proverbs 5:8
devotionals.title: Keep Your Distance
devotionals.prayer: Lord, give me the wisdom to set firm boundaries and the strength to keep them.

devotionals.question: Am I investing my "years" in things that build my life up or things that tear it down?
devotionals.reflection: Sin is a thief. It doesn't just take your peace; it steals your time, your reputation, and your vitality. Don't trade your years for a moment's impulse.
devotionals.scripture: ...lest you give your honor to others and your years to the merciless... — Proverbs 5:9
devotionals.title: The Cost of Honor
devotionals.prayer: Father, protect my testimony and my time. Help me to use my life for Your glory, not my own destruction.

devotionals.question: If I continue on my current trajectory, will I look back with regret or with gratitude?
devotionals.reflection: Wisdom starts at the finish line. If you look at the end of a path of disobedience, it always ends in groaning. Choose the path that ends in "Well done."
